articles.title: A Private Reprimand From The Fifth Circuit With Dissents To Make It Public
articles.content:
我始终对第十一巡回法院司法委员会决定将贝齐法官的谴责保密感到困惑。（是的，我知道她的名字，但贝齐法官这个称呼已经叫顺口了。）

正如我在昨天日落前快速撰写的帖子中所指出的，委员会认定存在明显的利益冲突，但公众却不知道哪位法官存在这种冲突。如果不知道法官的身份，诉讼当事人怎么可能判断某位法官是否应该被取消资格？我建议制定一项绝对规则：无论何时委员会认定存在明显的利益冲突，谴责必须公开。

怎么可能第十一巡回法院司法委员会的成员中没有一个人觉得有必要提出异议？事实上，第十一巡回法院甚至没有在备忘录上公布委员会成员的名单，只有首席法官普赖尔除外。我在网上找不到委员会现任成员的名单。我找到了2025年8月7日的另一份命令，其中列出了以下姓名：

威廉·普赖尔、乔丹、罗森鲍姆、吉尔·普赖尔、纽萨姆、布兰奇、格兰特、拉克、拉加亚和布拉舍，巡回法官；奥尔顿阿加、普罗克托、霍华德、加德纳、比弗斯托克、马克斯、贝克和温索尔，首席地区法官。

我理解成员资格每年可能都会有变化，所以我不确定哪些成员仍在任。

我非常乐意指出第五巡回法院的几位杰出成员选择了不同的道路。

2024年5月，第五巡回法院司法委员会对一名法官发出了保密谴责。事实很严重：

一个执法机构向美国地区法院法官提交了投诉。投诉称，被告法官在刑事案件密封的庭外会议上得知了执法部门反腐败调查的敏感和机密信息，并将这些信息透露给了一名家庭成员。申诉人称，该信息最终被转达给了调查目标，泄露行为使目标得以试图妨碍调查，并导致调查提前终止。调查目标被判犯有妨碍司法和其他罪行。

但“被告法官”没有被公开，因为他真的、真的非常抱歉，并承诺绝不再犯。（听起来很熟悉？）

在一封致特别委员会的信中，被告法官表示，他绝不会故意妨碍执法调查，他理解泄露政府调查机密信息的风险和后果。他还承诺今后避免此类泄露。特别委员会认为被告法官的陈述是真诚的，他承诺今后避免此类泄露的做法适当解决了投诉引发的担忧。

十九名司法委员会成员审议了此案：

里奇曼，首席法官；埃尔罗德、斯图尔特、科斯塔、威利特、何、邓肯、恩格尔哈特、奥尔德姆、威尔逊、扎伊尼、杰克逊、富特、米尔斯、里夫斯、金克纳德、罗森塔尔、吉尔斯特拉普和摩西。

但四名成员持异议，认为应该公开被告法官的姓名。

根据《司法行为和司法残疾诉讼程序规则》第24(a)(2)条的规定，现命令不披露被告法官的姓名。巡回法官珍妮弗·W·埃尔罗德、格雷格·J·科斯塔和詹姆斯·C·何以及地区法官卡尔顿·W·里夫斯会公开披露该投诉被告法官的姓名。

埃尔罗德、科斯塔、何和里夫斯法官做得好。对于那些关注此事的人来说，这些法官在意识形态光谱上处于非常不同的位置，但他们都同意这个重要的伦理问题：行为不端者应该受到公众监督。

委员会中的其他法官，我认识并尊重他们，却保持沉默。为什么？

趁此机会，我再提出另一个问题。目前尚不清楚原始投诉是何时提交的。但此案至少拖了2.5年。一个脚注列出了时间线：

标题中列名的法官在2021年11月经委员会审议并批准此事时是司法委员会成员，他们同意该决定。科斯塔法官于2022年8月31日从法院辞职。威利特、何、邓肯、富特和罗森塔尔法官的司法委员会任期于2021年12月31日届满。

这件事花了近三年时间才解决。在此期间，科斯塔法官已从法院辞职，其他成员也不再参与委员会工作。我支持通过立法改革为解决这些事务设定时限。公众需要及时了解不当行为。

《第五巡回法院发出带有异议的保密谴责，要求公开》一文首次出现在Reason.com上。
articles.summary:
I continue to remain confounded by the Eleventh Circuit's Judicial Council decision to make Judge Betsy's reprimand private. (Yes, I know her name, but Judge Betsy has stuck.)

As I noted in a post quickly written before sundown yesterday, the Council identified a clear conflict of interest, but left the public unaware of which judge possesses that conflict. How can litigants possibly determine if a judge should be disqualified if they do not know the identity of the judge? I would propose a per se rule: whenever the Council identifies a clear conflict of interest, the reprimand must be public.

How could it be that not a single member of the Eleventh Circuit Judicial council felt compelled to dissent? Indeed, the Eleventh Circuit does not even publish the names of the members of the Council on the memorandum, other than Chief Judge Pryor. I've been unable to find the current members of the Council anywhere online. I found another order from August 7, 2025 that lists the following names:

WILLIAM PRYOR, JORDAN, ROSENBAUM, JILL PRYOR, NEWSOM, BRANCH, GRANT, LUCK, LAGOA, and BRASHER, Circuit Judges; ALTONAGA, PROCTOR, HOWARD, GARDNER, BEAVERSTOCK, MARKS, BAKER, and WINSOR, Chief District Judges.*

I understand this membership may change every year, so I'm not sure which members remain.

I would gladly point to several distinguished members of the Fifth Circuit who took a different path.

In May 2024, the Fifth Circuit Judicial Council issued a private reprimand of a judge. The facts were serious:

A law enforcement agency filed a complaint against a United States district court judge. The complaint alleges that the subject judge revealed sensitive and confidential information regarding a law-enforcement public corruption investigation, which the judge learned in a sealed bench conference in a criminal case pending before him, to a family member. The complainant alleges that the information was eventually relayed to the target of the investigation, and that the disclosure allowed the target to attempt to obstruct the investigation and brought the investigation to an early end. The target of the investigation was convicted of obstruction of justice and other offenses.

But the "subject judge" was not outed because he was really, really sorry, and promised it would never happen again. (Sound familiar?)

In a letter to the Special Committee, the subject judge stated that he would never intentionally interfere with a law enforcement investigation and he understands the risks and consequences of disclosing confidential information about government investigations. He also committed to avoid such disclosures in the future. The Special Committee found the judge's representations to be sincere and that his commitment to avoid such disclosures in the future appropriately addresses their concerns raised by the complaint.

Nineteen members of the Judicial Council considered this case:

RICHMAN, Chief Judge, ELROD, STEWART, COSTA, WILLETT, HO, DUNCAN, ENGELHARDT, OLDHAM, WILSON, ZAINEY, JACKSON, FOOTE, MILLS, REEVES, KINKEADE, ROSENTHAL, GILSTRAP, and MOSES

But four members dissented, and would have disclosed the name of the "subject judge"

Pursuant to Rule 24(a)(2) of the Rules for Judicial-Conduct and Judicial-Disability Proceedings, it is ordered that the name of the subject judge not be disclosed. Circuit Judges Jennifer W. Elrod, Gregg J. Costa and James C. Ho and District Judge Carlton W. Reeves would publicly disclose the name of the Judge who is the subject of the complaint.

Good for Judges Elrod, Costa, Ho, and Reeves. And for those keeping score at home, these judges are on very different positions along the ideological spectrum, yet they all agree on this important ethical issue: people who misbehave should receive public scrutiny.

Other judges on the Council, who I know and respect, were silent. Why?

While I'm at it, let me raise another issue. It is unclear when the original complaint was filed. But this case stretched at least 2.5 years. A footnote lays out this timeline:

The judges named in the caption were members of the Judicial Council when this matter was considered and approved by the Council in November 2021, and they concurred in the decision. Judge Costa resigned from the Court effective August 31, 2022. The Judicial Council terms of Judges Willett, Ho, Duncan, Foote, and Rosenthal expired December 31, 2021.

It took nearly three years to settle this matter. In the interim, Judge Costa had resigned from the court, and other members were no longer participating on the Council. I favor a legislative reform to put a clock on settling these matters. The public needs to know about misconduct in a timely fashion.

The post A Private Reprimand From The Fifth Circuit With Dissents To Make It Public appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Trump Administration Will Appeal Ruling Requiring Tariff Refunds
articles.content:
特朗普政府昨日提交的一份动议显示，他们打算对美国国际贸易法院的一项命令提出上诉。该命令要求向所有支付了特朗普根据1977年《国际紧急经济权力法》（IEEPA）征收的非法关税的企业退还税款。这些关税此前已被最高法院裁定无效——这起案件由我协助提起并参与诉讼（与自由司法中心及其他机构合作）。

今年3月，当国际贸易法院法官伊顿发布命令时，我曾认为政府可能会以该命令与最高法院在特朗普诉CASA一案（去年作出判决）中不明智地对全面禁令设置的限制相矛盾为由提出上诉。但是，在随后近三个月的时间里，政府并未提出上诉，实际上甚至建立了一个关税退税系统，开始向遭受非法关税之害的企业发放退款。维克多·施瓦茨是V.O.S. Selections公司的所有者，也是我们案件的首席客户，他已经是获得退款的人之一。

政府现在打算辩称，只有提起诉讼的企业才有权获得赔偿。在我看来，这种情况说明了为什么在某些类型的案件中我们需要全面禁令。我们有成千上万的企业和其他进口商被迫支付了超过1660亿美元的非法关税。要求他们各自提起诉讼将是巨大的时间和金钱浪费，对规模较小、资金不足的组织来说更是沉重的负担。这也将大大延迟部分退款的发放，使纳税人最终支付利息。

在我之前一篇关于退款问题的文章中，我指出了法官伊顿在此案中发布全面禁令是正确的其他原因，最主要的是特朗普诉CASA一案只适用于根据1789年《司法法案》及其后续法案发布的禁令，而不适用于国际贸易法院权威来源的1980年独立管辖法规。

正如我在之前的文章中也指出的那样，即使完全退还非法征收的关税也无法完全弥补它们造成的损害。除其他外，这些退款无法补偿不得不支付更高价格的消费者，也无法补偿失去销售或遭受投资减少、与供应商关系中断的企业。这些因素都反对暂缓执行针对非法关税征收的禁令——法院在我们的案件中错误地这样做了（但国际贸易法院在正在进行的第122条关税案件中最近作出了相反的裁决）。

政府已表明不愿向所有受害者全额退款，这是另一个反对暂缓执行针对第122条关税禁令的理由，也是反对暂缓执行行政当局可能试图征收的任何其他非法关税的理由。

与此同时，目前尚不清楚这一上诉需要多长时间才能解决，以及在此期间关税退税是否会继续。我希望联邦巡回上诉法院和最高法院（如果案件到达那里的话）能作出不利于政府的裁决，并在诉讼继续进行期间拒绝暂缓执行法官伊顿的裁决。

补充说明：抛开程序技术问题不谈，政府有道德和法律义务退还所有款项，这一点不难理解。正如我在之前一篇文章中指出的：

“特朗普政府可以简单地放弃这场法律斗争，向所有被迫支付非法关税的人退还税款，从而轻松解决退款问题。这并不难做到。政府有所有付款和付款人的记录。计算利息也不困难。政府可以向所有有权获得退款的人进行电子支付或邮寄支票。”

归根结底，政府非法扣押了数十亿美元，因此必须归还。如果我非法和不公正地拿走你的财产，我有义务归还它，并支付利息。联邦政府这样做时也适用同样的原则。你不必成为法律理论家或关税专家就能理解这个简单的道理。

注：正如我之前已经指出的，我不再是V.O.S. Selections法律团队的成员，因为我的角色在最高法院作出裁决后已经结束。因此，我没有参与关税诉讼的退款阶段。

《特朗普政府将对要求关税退款的裁决提出上诉》一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
In a motion filed yesterday, the Trump Administration indicated their intent to appeal the US Court of International Trade's  order requiring payment of refunds to all those businesses who paid illegal tariffs imposed by Trump using the the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA). The IEEPA tariffs were invalidated by the Supreme Court, in a case I helped bring and litigate (along with the Liberty Justice Center, and others).

When Judge Eaton of the Court of International Trade (CIT) issued his order in March, I thought the administration might appeal on the grounds that it is inconsistent with the Supreme Court's ill-advised strictures against universal injunctions in Trump v. CASA, Inc. (decided last year). But, in the intervening nearly three-month period, the administration did not appeal, and indeed even set up a tariff refund system that has begun issuing payments to businesses victimized by the illegal tariffs. Victor Schwartz, owner of V.O.S. Selections, the lead client in our case, is one of those who have already gotten a refund.

The administration now plans to argue that only those businesses who filed lawsuits are entitled to recovery. To my mind, this situation illustrates why we need universal injunctions in some types of cases. We have many thousands of businesses and other importers who were forced to pay over $166 billion in illegal tariffs. Requiring each of them to file their own lawsuits would be a huge waste of time and money, and major burden for smaller, less well-funded organizations.  It would also significantly delay some of the payments, leaving taxpayers on the hook for interest payments when the refunds are finally issued.

In a previous post about the refund issue, I noted additional reasons why Judge Eaton was right to issue a universal injunction here, most notably that Trump v. CASA only applies to injunctions issued under the Judiciary Act of 1789 and its successors, not to the separate 1980 jurisdictional statute from which the Court of International Trade gets its authority.

As I have also noted in previous posts, even complete refunds for illegally collected tariffs cannot fully remedy the harm they cause. Among other things, they cannot compensate consumers who had to pay higher prices, or businesses who lost sales or suffered from diminished investment and disrupted relationships with suppliers. These factors argue against staying injunctions against the collection of illegal tariffs, as the courts mistakenly did in our case (but the CIT recently ruled the other way in the ongoing Section 122 tariff case).

The government's demonstrated unwillingness to issue complete refunds to all the victims is another reason not to stay injunctions against the Section 122 tariffs, or any other illegal tariffs the executive might try to impose.

In the meantime, it is unclear how long this appeal will take to be resolved, and whether tariff refunds will continue in the meantime. I hope the the Federal Circuit and the Supreme Court (should the case get there) rule against the administration, and refuse to stay Judge Eaton's ruling while the litigation continues.

UPDATE: Technical procedural issues aside, the administration has a moral and legal duty to refund all the money, and it should not be hard to see why. As I noted in a previous post:

[T]he Trump Administration can easily resolve the refund issue simply by giving up this legal fight and issuing refunds to all those forced to pay the illegal tariffs. That would not be hard to do. The government has a record of all the payments and who made them. Calculating interest also is not difficult. The government could just make electronic payments or send checks to all those entitled to them.

Ultimately, the government illegally seized billions of dollars and therefore must pay them back.  If I unjustly and illegally take your property, I have a duty to give it back, and pay interest. The same principle applies when the federal government does it. You don't have to be a legal theorist or a tariff expert to grasp this simple point.

NOTE: As I have previously noted, I am no longer a member of the V.O.S. Selections legal team, because my role ended after the Supreme Court issued its decision. Thus, I am not  involved in the refund phase of the tariff litigation.

The post Trump Administration Will Appeal Ruling Requiring Tariff Refunds appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Libel Suit by "King of Vape" Against N.Y. Post, Over Allegations of Misconduct and Anti-Israel Actions, Thrown Out
articles.content:
佛罗里达州南区联邦地区法院法官谢里·波斯特·查佩尔昨日就施里特诉纽约邮报控股公司案作出裁决：

本案为诽谤诉讼。原告在佛罗里达州西南部经营17家电子烟零售店，使用“电子烟之王”商标。克里斯滕森为《纽约邮报》撰写并发表了一篇关于原告的文章，标题为《佛罗里达“仇恨以色列的电子烟之王”面临两党联合打击，整治非法、儿童友好的中国电子烟销售》。该文章据称包含多项关于原告的虚假陈述，导致原告遭受损害。

在就先前驳回请求作出裁定时，法院认定原告为公众人物，须适用实际恶意标准。原因何在？因为原告自己在诉状中的陈述表明他是一名公众人物。

具体而言，在第二次修改起诉状（以及此前的每份起诉状）中，原告声称他于2000年移民美国之前，“是加沙地区受人尊敬的记者，为《纽约时报》、路透社和哥伦比亚广播公司新闻部撰稿，1993年因其勇敢的工作获得约翰·R·奥布查恩国际新闻自由奖”。他还声称与“尊敬的以色列教授”合著了《超越起义》一书，该书“因对理解以色列-巴勒斯坦冲突的人道影响作出贡献而获得认可”。他反复声称被告在发表诽谤性陈述时怀有实际恶意。

法院认为，这些陈述表明他是公众人物，因此他必须指控被告存在实际恶意。由于原告未能合理地提出此类指控，法院驳回了第二次修改起诉状，并允许其修改以充分主张实际恶意。

原告并未遵循法院的指示，充分主张实际恶意，而是试图耍小聪明。在第三次修改起诉状中，原告删除了法院在认定其为公众人物时所依据的所有事实陈述。现在他主张，由于第三次修改起诉状中没有任何内容表明他是公众人物，他无需主张实际恶意。但这种取巧的诉状策略行不通。

当法院在驳回起诉后准许原告修改起诉状时，原告并不能随心所欲地修改。修改的范围仅限于法院允许和指示的范围——即纠正已指出的诉状缺陷。虽然法院通常会根据《联邦民事诉讼规则》第15条（a）款准许修改，但在原告行为不诚信的情况下，法院无需如此。

原告的修改遗漏了表明其为公众人物的陈述，不仅不符合获准修改以增加实际恶意事实陈述的授权范围，而且明显是规避法院关于其为公众人物之裁定的蓄意举动。准许这种不诚信策略将允许原告“操纵诉讼进程”。简而言之，原告在第二次修改起诉状中的陈述已经暴露了他是公众人物。他不能为了维持案件存活而试图将事实重新掩盖。

在这方面，原告仍未合理主张被告存在实际恶意的相关事实。他甚至没有尝试。法院将他试图回避公众人物身份、避免主张实际恶意的行为解读为他承认自己无法做到这一点。由于法院已经给原告一次机会来弥补这一缺陷，法院驳回原告的起诉，并不得再行修改。

还有一点。原告在答辩状中还根据第54条（b）款请求法院重新考虑先前关于其为公众人物的裁定。但原告不能通过答辩状寻求积极救济……“请求法院作出裁定必须通过动议提出。”

被告代理律师为：本杰明·约瑟夫·泰勒和斯科特·D·庞塞（霍兰与奈特律师事务所），以及切尔西·T·凯利、劳拉·R·汉德曼和莉娜·M·查尔顿（戴维斯·赖特·特雷梅恩律师事务所）。
articles.summary:
From Judge Sheri Polster Chappell (M.D. Fla.) yesterday in Shriteh v. NYP Holdings, Inc.:

This is a defamation case. Plaintiff operates seventeen vape retail stores in southwest Florida under the trademark name "the King of Vape." Christenson, writing for the New York Post, authored and published an article about Plaintiff titled, "Florida's Israel-hating 'King of Vape' Faces Bipartisan Crackdown on Sale of Illicit, Kid-Friendly Chinese E-cigs." The article allegedly included several false statements about Plaintiff resulting in damages….

When ruling on a prior motion to dismiss, the Court determined Plaintiff is a public figure subject to the actual malice standard. Why? Because Plaintiff's own allegations indicated he was a public figure.

Specifically, in the Second Amended Complaint (and each preceding complaint), Plaintiff alleged that prior to immigrating to the United States in 2000, he was "a respected journalist working in Gaza and reporting for the New York Times, Reuters, and CBS News, whose courageous efforts were recognized in 1993 when he received the John R. Aubuchon International Freedom of the Press Award." He also alleged he co-authored a book, Beyond Intifada, with "esteemed Israeli professors" which "has been recognized for its contribution to understanding the human impact of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict." And he repeatedly alleged that Defendants issued and made the defamatory statements with actual malice.

The Court held that these allegations indicate he is a public figure and, thus, he must allege Defendants acted with actual malice. And because Plaintiff failed to plausibly do so, the Court dismissed the Second-Amended Complaint with leave to amend to adequately allege actual malice.

Rather than comply with the Court's directive and sufficiently allege actual malice, Plaintiff tried to get clever. In the Third-Amended Complaint, Plaintiff removed all factual allegations the Court relied upon when determining Plaintiff is a public figure. And he now argues he does not need to allege actual malice because nothing in the Third Amended Complaint suggests he is a public figure. But such creative pleading will not fly.

When a court permits a plaintiff leave to amend after dismissing a complaint, a plaintiff does not have carte blanche to amend as he sees fit. Rather, the amendment is limited to the scope permitted and instructed by the court—i.e., to correct identified pleading deficiencies. And although a court generally will grant leave to amend under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 15(a), the court need not do so if the plaintiff is acting in bad faith.

Not only was Plaintiff's amendment omitting allegations that implicate him as a public figure incompliant with the permitted leave to add factual allegations of actual malice, but the amendment was clearly a calculated effort to end-run the Court's order concluding Plaintiff is a public figure. Permitting such bad-faith tactics would allow Plaintiff "to manipulate the course of litigation." Put simply, Plaintiff's allegations in the second amended complaint let the cat out of the bag that he is a public figure. He cannot put the cat back into the bag in the hope of keeping his case alive.

On that score, Plaintiff still has not plausibly alleged facts suggesting Defendants acted with actual malice. He does not even try. And the Court construes his attempt to dodge status as a public figure and avoid pleading actual malice as a concession that he cannot do so. Because the Court already provided Plaintiff an opportunity to cure this deficiency, the Court dismisses Plaintiff's complaint with prejudice.

One more point. In his response, Plaintiff also moves under Rule 54(b) for the Court to reconsider its prior holding that he is a public figure. But he cannot seek affirmative relief through a response brief…. "A request for a court order must be made by motion." …

Benjamin Joseph Tyler and Scott D. Ponce (Holland & Knight LLP) and Chelsea T. Kelly, Laura R. Handman, and Leena M. Charlton (Davis Wright Tremaine LLP) represent defendants.

The post Libel Suit by "King of Vape" Against N.Y. Post, Over Allegations of Misconduct and Anti-Israel Actions, Thrown Out appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: The Satanic Temple Loses Libel Suit Against Newsweek Over "Accounts of Sexual Abuse Being Covered up" Allegation
articles.content:
2024年2月第二巡回上诉法院在"撒旦教神殿诉新闻周刊数字公司案"（The Satanic Temple, Inc. v. Newsweek Digital LLC）中的判决，由内森法官主笔，卡巴内斯法官和梅里亚姆法官参与合议。

2021年10月，新闻周刊数字公司（Newsweek）发表了一篇题为《狂欢、骚扰、欺诈：撒旦教神殿遭受指控和诉讼》的文章。该文由驻华盛顿记者朱莉娅·杜因撰写，详细描述了撒旦教神殿成员与其领导层之间的若干内部纷争。撒旦教神殿随即起诉新闻周刊诽谤，声称文中许多陈述虚假且具有诽谤性。案件进入即决审判时，仅剩一项陈述处于争议之中：一位前撒旦教神殿成员关于组织内部"有关性虐待的指控被以超出逸闻轶事的方式掩盖"的引述（以下简称"掩盖引述"）。

在即决审判中，地区法院认定，任何理性陪审员都无法认为新闻周刊在发表前知道或对这一引述的真实性持严重怀疑态度，因此批准了对该杂志的有利即决判决。……鉴于纽约州选择提供比第一修正案要求更大的诽谤被告保护，我们维持原判……

纽约州的反SLAPP法规要求诽谤原告在就涉及"公共利益"事项的公开陈述提起诉讼时证明"实际恶意"（即明知虚假或 reckless 虚假），无论陈述是否涉及公众人物。在这一方面，该法规对诽谤被告的保护比第一修正案更为有力。法院认定，反SLAPP法规的这项规定属于实体性规定而非程序性规定，因此适用于联邦法院。法院推理如下：

[纽约]州的反SLAPP保护适用于与此相关的"在向公众开放的场所或公共论坛中进行的、与公共利益问题相关的任何交流"。[...]涉案诽谤性陈述发表在新闻周刊网站上，这无疑是一个"公共论坛"。[...]而且该文陈述涉及"公共利益问题"。从其被指控具有诽谤性的角度阅读，该陈述暗示撒旦教神殿——一个拥有超过50万成员的全国性组织——惯常性地掩盖或未能处理内部性虐待投诉。纽约法院已认定经修订的反SLAPP法规适用于类似案件，以及公共利益远不那么明确的案件，参见Aristocrat Plastic Surgery, P.C. v. Silva（纽约上诉法院2022年）（认定原告对整形外科医生的在线评价涉及公共利益问题，因为这些评价"向潜在患者提供了信息"）。我们还注意到，我们必须"广义地解释'公共利益'……包括"除纯私人事务之外的任何主题"。《纽约州民事权利法》第76-a(1)(d)条……

撒旦教神殿认为，在原告不是公众人物的情况下，反SLAPP法规不能适用，但这一论点被法规的明文规定所否定——该法规并不包含"公众人物"要求。{地区法院在即决审判中未审理撒旦教神殿作为公众人物的身份问题，因此未作裁决。在上诉中，双方均未就此问题进行详细辩论。因此，我们不对撒旦教神殿是否属于第一修正案意义上的公众人物发表意见。}

最后，撒旦教神殿诉诸一般第一修正案原则并无帮助。撒旦教神殿主张，我们必须"区分那些值得第一修正案保护的主张和那些不值得保护的主张"——而它声称，眼前的陈述属于不值得保护的一类。但这并非我们的职责。"各州可以自行定义针对诽谤性虚假陈述损害私人个体的出版商的适当责任标准"，而且如果它们愿意的话，"可以提供比联邦法律更大的人权保护"。纽约州已作出选择：在涉及"在公共论坛上就公共利益问题发表的陈述"的案件中，纽约选择适用比宪法原本要求的更高过错标准。我们没有理由质疑这一选择；我们的职责是适用它……

法院随后认定撒旦教神殿未能提供足够证据证明"实际恶意"：

要证明实际恶意，撒旦教神殿必须证明"作者在发表前实际上对出版物的真实性持严重怀疑，或高度意识到其可能为虚假"。撒旦教神殿声称已就新闻周刊对掩盖引述真实性的"严重怀疑"建立了可审理的事实争议，并提出若干论点支持：（1）新闻周刊未能遵守其自身的编辑标准；（2）杜因和库珀对该组织的偏见；（3）新闻周刊使用了一个敌意的假名消息来源；（4）新闻周刊在事实核查方面的不足。即使对记录作出最有利于撒旦教神殿的解释，实际恶意是一个"相当高的过错标准"，我们认定上述任何一项，无论单独还是组合，都不足以产生关于新闻周刊实际恶意的可审理事实争议。

首先，假设新闻周刊确实违反了自身的编辑准则，例如在撰写刑事指控报道时未能做到"具体和完整"，这种违规行为并不构成实际恶意。即使是"严重违背负责任出版商通常遵守的调查和报道标准的高度不合理行为"，也不能单独确立实际恶意。[...]"记者是否遵守新闻标准"在 actual malice 争议中"几乎不具有证据价值"[...]。撒旦教神殿主张，即使遵守行业范围内的新闻标准不足以确立实际恶意，遵守自身标准更能说明新闻机构对真相的 reckless 漠视。我们不同意这一观点。无论新闻采集标准是行业惯例还是新闻周刊自己的，撒旦教神殿的论点都属于过失范畴，而"仅证明过失不足以确立实际恶意"。

同样，库珀和杜因据称对撒旦教神殿怀有的偏见——……假设他们确实持有这种偏见——也不足以证明新闻周刊对掩盖引述具有实际恶意。"实际恶意"作为过错标准"不应与源于恶意或怨恨的动机混为一谈"。因此，"仅表明恶意或'恶意'的普通含义不足以满足该标准"。虽然我们曾说过"恶意证据与其他表明被告 reckless 漠视真相的间接证据相结合"可能支持实际恶意的认定，"但仅凭恶意证据……不足以"作出该认定[……]。

第三，新闻周刊依赖斯特兰奇作为消息来源不支持实际恶意的认定。除非报道完全基于该来源未经核实的匿名报告，或者"有明显理由怀疑信息提供者真实性或其报告准确性"的情况下，记者使用匿名——或本案中的假名——消息来源本身不构成实际恶意。在本案中，斯特兰奇关于"有关性虐待的指控被以超出逸闻轶事的方式掩盖"的报告得到了多个其他来源的印证，因此杜因没有"明显理由"怀疑他或该陈述的真实性。撒旦教神殿进一步主张，新闻周刊依赖一个"敌意的"来源斯特兰奇——后者因与组织方向不一致而离开——表明其 reckless 漠视真相。但我们在"国际科学教教会诉贝哈尔案"（第二巡回法院2001年）中驳回了类似论点，在该案中，被告记者依赖原告组织的"前高级成员"，此人此前曾"通过策划虚假的死亡威胁来陷害[该组织]"。即使面对这种明显的偏见，我们仍然认定，在记者对该来源的陈述进行了"大量印证"的情况下，仅凭对消息来源的依赖不足以确立足以规避即决审判的实际恶意。同样，新闻周刊对斯特兰奇这一获得"大量印证"的引述的使用，在本案中也不能支持实际恶意的认定。

第四，也是最后，新闻周刊未能独立核实掩盖引述，也未能就此向格雷夫斯征求回应，这些都不足以产生关于实际恶意的可审理事实争议。无可争议的是，库珀依赖杜因核查她自己的文章，而杜因在采访中并未专门就斯特兰奇的掩盖引述询问格雷夫斯。也许这些行为是过失的，但两者都不能表明对掩盖引述真实性的 reckless 漠视。仅仅调查不足不足以证明实际恶意；我们曾说过，"对于尚无明显理由怀疑报道准确性的出版商，不要求其启动可能产生这种怀疑的调查"。撒旦教神殿没有指出任何此类本应促使新闻周刊进一步调查的"明显理由"。

总之，新闻周刊在发表前可能确实不知道斯特兰奇的掩盖引述是否属实。但"不知道某事是否为真与高度意识到其可能为假之间存在关键区别"，"只有后者才能在诽谤诉讼中确立 reckless 漠视"。本案记录中没有任何内容支持可以推断新闻周刊在发布前高度意识到掩盖引述是虚假的——如果它确实是虚假的话。

因此，我们同意地区法院的结论：任何合理陪审员都不能认定新闻周刊在发表前知道或对掩盖引述的虚假性持严重怀疑态度……

卡梅伦·斯特拉彻和萨拉·特索里耶罗（卡梅伦·斯特拉彻律师事务所）代表新闻周刊。
articles.summary:
From Thursday's decision in The Satanic Temple, Inc. v. Newsweek Digital LLC, written by Judge Alison Nathan and joined by Judges José Cabranes and Sarah Merriam:

In October 2021, Newsweek Digital LLC (Newsweek) published an article entitled "Orgies, Harassment, Fraud: Satanic Temple Rocked by Accusations, Lawsuit." The article, written by Washington-based journalist Julia Duin, details several internal disputes between members of The Satanic Temple (the Temple) and its leadership. The Satanic Temple responded by suing Newsweek for libel, claiming that many statements in the article were false and defamatory. By the time the case reached summary judgment, only one statement remained at issue: A quote from a former Satanic Temple member describing "[a]ccounts of sexual abuse being covered up in ways that were more than anecdotal" (the cover-up quote) within the organization.

On summary judgment, the district court concluded that no rational juror could find Newsweek either knew or entertained serious doubts that this quote was false prior to publication, and therefore it granted summary judgment in favor of the magazine…. [I]n recognition of New York's choice to offer greater protection to defamation defendants than the First Amendment requires, we affirm ….

New York's anti-SLAPP statute requires libel plaintiffs to show "actual malice" (i.e., knowing or reckless falsehood) when they sue over publicly made statements on matters of "public interest," whether or not the statements are about public figures. In this respect, it protects libel defendants more strongly than First Amendment law does. The court held that this provision of the anti-SLAPP statute is substantive rather than procedural, and thus applies in federal court. And it reasoned,

[New York] state's anti-SLAPP protections apply to, as relevant here, "any communication in a place open to the public or a public forum in connection with an issue of public interest[.]" … [T]he allegedly defamatory statement was printed on Newsweek's website, which is indisputably a "public forum." … [And] the article statement addresses "an issue of public interest[.]"Read in its allegedly defamatory manner, the statement suggests that The Satanic Temple—a nationwide organization with over 500,000 members—habitually concealed or failed to address internal complaints of sexual abuse. New York courts have concluded that the amended anti-SLAPP statute applies in similar cases, as well as in cases where the public interest is far less clear, see Aristocrat Plastic Surgery, P.C. v. Silva (N.Y. App. Div. 2022) (concluding that plaintiff's online reviews of plastic surgeon addressed an issue of public interest because they "provide[d] information to potential patients"). We are mindful, too, that we must construe "[p]ublic interest … broadly," to include "any subject other than a purely private matter." N.Y. Civ. Rights Law § 76-a(1)(d)….

[T]o the extent The Satanic Temple argues that the anti-SLAPP statute cannot apply when the plaintiff is not a public figure, that argument is belied by the plain text of the statute, which does not contain a "public figure" requirement. {The district court was not presented with the question of The Satanic Temple's status as a public figure on summary judgment and thus did not resolve it. On appeal, neither party has briefed the issue. Accordingly, we express no opinion about whether The Satanic Temple is a public figure for First Amendment purposes.}

Finally, the Temple's appeal to general First Amendment principles is unhelpful. The Satanic Temple claims that we must "delineate those charges which deserve First Amendment protection from those that don't"—and the instant statement, it claims, is one that does not deserve such protection. But that is not our job. "[T]he States may define for themselves the appropriate standard of liability for a publisher … of defamatory falsehood injurious to a private individual," and they "are free to offer greater protection to individual rights than federal law affords" if they so choose. New York has made its choice: In cases concerning statements made in "a public forum in connection with an issue of public interest," New York has chosen to impose a higher standard of fault than the Constitution otherwise requires. It is not our job to second-guess that choice; it is our job to apply it….

The court then concluded that The Satanic Temple hadn't offered enough evidence to show "actual malice":

To show actual malice, The Satanic Temple must demonstrate "that the author in fact entertained serious doubts as to the truth of his publication or acted with a high degree of awareness of probable falsity" prior to publication. The Satanic Temple claims that it established a triable issue of fact as to Newsweek's "serious doubts" about the cover-up quote's truth, and offers several arguments as support: (1) Newsweek's failure to follow its own editorial standards, (2) Duin and Cooper's bias against the organization, (3) Newsweek's use of a hostile and pseudonymous source, and (4) Newsweek's shortcomings in its fact-checking. Even construing the record in the light most favorable to The Satanic Temple, actual malice is a "decidedly high standard of culpability," and we conclude that none of the above, taken alone or in combination, is enough to raise a triable issue of fact as to Newsweek's actual malice.

First, assuming arguendo that Newsweek violated its own editorial guidelines by, for example, failing to be "specific and complete" when writing about criminal allegations, that failure does not constitute actual malice. Even "highly unreasonable conduct constituting an extreme departure from the standards of investigation and reporting ordinarily adhered to by responsible publishers" cannot alone establish actual malice…. "[W]hether [the reporter] complied with journalistic standards is of little evidentiary value" in an actual malice dispute …. The Satanic Temple contends that, even if compliance with industry-wide journalistic standards is not enough to establish actual malice, compliance with one's own standards is more probative of a news outlet's reckless disregard for the truth. We disagree. Whether the newsgathering standards were industry-wide norms or Newsweek's own, The Satanic Temple's argument sounds in negligence, and "proof of mere negligence does not suffice to establish actual malice."

Likewise, Cooper and Duin's alleged bias against The Satanic Temple—… assuming arguendo that they possessed such a bias—is not enough to establish that Newsweek acted with actual malice as to the cover-up quote. "Actual malice" as a standard of fault "should not be confused with … a motive arising from spite or ill will." Therefore, the standard "is not satisfied merely through a showing of ill will or 'malice' in the ordinary sense of the term." While we have said that "[e]vidence of ill will combined with other circumstantial evidence indicating that the defendant acted with reckless disregard of the truth" may support a finding of actual malice, "[s]tanding alone, … evidence of ill will is not sufficient." …

Third, Newsweek's reliance on Strange as a source does not support a finding of actual malice. A journalist's use of an anonymous—or, in this case, pseudonymous—source does not establish actual malice unless the story is, for example, "based wholly on an unverified anonymous" report from that source, or where "there are obvious reasons to doubt the veracity of the informant or the accuracy of his reports." Here, Strange's report of "accounts of sexual abuse being covered up in ways that were more than anecdotal" was corroborated by multiple other sources, such that Duin had no "obvious reason[ ]" to doubt him or the statement's veracity. The Satanic Temple further argues that Newsweek demonstrated a reckless disregard for the truth by relying on a "hostile" source in Strange, who left the organization due to disagreements with its direction. But we rejected a similar argument in Church of Scientology Int'l v. Behar (2d Cir. 2001), in which the defendant reporter relied on "a former high-ranking" member of plaintiff's organization who had previously "tried to frame the [organization] … by staging a phony death threat." Even in the face of that overt bias, we held that the reporter's reliance on the source could not establish actual malice sufficient to elude summary judgment in light of the reporter's "considerable corroboration" of the source's account. Similarly, Newsweek's use of the "considerabl[y] corroborat[ed]" quote from Strange cannot support a finding of actual malice here.

Fourth, and finally, neither Newsweek's failure to independently fact check the cover-up quote nor its failure to ask Greaves for his response to it is sufficient to create a triable issue of fact on actual malice. It is uncontroverted that Cooper relied on Duin to fact-check her own article, and that Duin did not specifically ask Greaves about Strange's cover-up quote during their interview. Perhaps those actions were negligent, but neither demonstrates a reckless disregard for the truth of the cover-up quote. A news outlet's insufficient investigation alone is not enough to demonstrate actual malice; we have said that "a publisher who does not already have obvious reasons to doubt the accuracy of a story is not required to initiate an investigation that might plant such doubt." The Satanic Temple points to no such "obvious reasons" that ought to have prompted Newsweek to investigate further.

In sum, it may well be the case that Newsweek was unaware whether Strange's cover-up quote was indeed true prior to publication. But "there is a critical difference between not knowing whether something is true and being highly aware that it is probably false," and "[o]nly the latter establishes reckless disregard in a defamation action." Nothing on this record supports an inference that Newsweek was highly aware that the coverup quote was false—if indeed it was—before hitting publish.

We therefore agree with the district court: No reasonable jury could find that Newsweek either knew or entertained serious doubts that the cover-up quote was false prior to publication….

Cameron Stracher and Sara Tesoriero (Cameron Stracher, PLLC) represent Newsweek.

The post The Satanic Temple Loses Libel Suit Against Newsweek Over "Accounts of Sexual Abuse Being Covered up" Allegation appeared first on Reason.com.
